examples.prompt: Write about a recent retreat or getaway where you were able to focus on writing or another creative pursuit.
examples.reference:
•5:00-7:00 p.m. Fresh air, walking, and creative well refilling. Whether that was strolling through an art gallery, walking around looking at historic buildings, churches, and sculptures - it was a brain break.

•7:00-8:30 p.m. Dinner. Naturally, we'd end up talking about our work, which led to life, family, and all the things business friends talk about.

•9:00-10:00 p.m. Relax, unwind, then bed.

I didn't have a word count goal going into the retreat, but I had about six ideas bulleted out that I needed to flesh out. I guessed that each of the six ideas would be roughly the length of a blog post. It turns out I was right.

•After day one, hour one, I had written 3,557 words (which counted the bullets I had outlined ahead of time). My most productive session!

•By the end of day one, I had 4,700 words.

•By 10:00 a.m. on day two, I had 5,680 words. And I spent much of the second day revisiting what I wrote on day one, which meant tweaking and adding stuff.

•By 11:00 a.m. (lunchtime), I had 6,231 words.

•On day three, I had a three-hour writing marathon in the middle of the day for my community (which meant they were writing and I was coaching) and had to scoot out early to get to my son's playoff hockey game, so I didn't write as much as I thought I would.

•Total three-day writing time for my business: eleven hours.

•Total new words for my business: 8,200 words.

When I first saw the total word count and time invested for three days, my ego wanted to feel crappy about it - I should have prepared more, pushed harder, and written more.

But I stopped that mind chatter. I did more than enough. At this time, I wasn't yet aligning my writing activities with nature's rhythms. We had some great conversations, and I had to think through some business decisions as these came up in my writing. And there were 8,200 words to use in my business that didn't exist before.

Here's an estimated breakdown of my total costs:

•Airbnb for three nights (my portion): $260

•Gas: $30

•Parking: $100

•Lunches and dinners: $400 (this was the highest because I like food and picked up the tab for at least one dinner)

Total cost: $790.

You could do it for much more or much less. Travel costs were low, and food costs were relatively higher.

My biggest worry going into this writing retreat: If I was investing all this time and energy in this trip, I wanted to be sure I'd get more writing done than I would have at home.

Since I didn't plan much in advance, I worried that I'd have too much client work to do or that I wouldn't feel inspired, clear, or even have the stamina to write anything useful. Next time, I'll plan it further in advance and make sure to block my calendar, so I have complete focus.

One of us didn't write for the specific project we were working on at all on day one - and that's okay.

After our second Pomodoro sprint, as I was pouring a third cup of coffee (the cups were tiny), one friend looked up from her laptop and said, "I'm not writing what I'm supposed to."

Without missing a beat, I replied, "That's okay." Because it is okay. She was writing something - that's the important part. She was journaling, emailing, and doing other writing.

It was so powerful to see living proof that my belief that writer's block is BS was accurate. She had some stuff to sort out in her mind and heart before she could get to her intended project.

By the afternoon, she was ready to work on her project, and by day two, she wrote like an intuitive MOFO.

I hope this inspires you to create a little writing retreat of your own - to start your website, a book, a screenplay, a few months of blog content, a bunch of poems, or whatever it is you want to write.

Extended vacations can turn into a writing retreat. I've kick-started lots of projects on planes sans Wi-Fi. You'll love how Mairéad, a writing community member, turned travel into a daily writing habit.

Before 2020, we used to travel for months from Ireland to mainland Europe in our camper van. I wrote a blog post most days. I cannot understand where I found the time. Travel in a van has lots of extra chores because it's not connected to water or electricity, it has no dishwasher or washing machine, and on damp days there's no suitable place to hang your wet clothes. It can get very uncomfortable.

I went on a peer-led writing retreat last week in Philly with two business friends.

We made our own working retreat because, just like you, I have trouble getting my writing done - especially with all the responsibilities that come with running a household and being the primary breadwinner for my family. And as a copywriter who works one-on-one with clients, there's no such thing as taking time off without work piling up.

The retreat was our way to take some of that responsibility off our plates. We planned it around a specific goal we were all working toward - writing a series of blog posts on our websites. Each of us had an outline and some rough draft material already in place, so before heading out, we had a call to discuss the plan, talk about what we each wanted to accomplish over the course of the weekend.

There was one big difference though - I didn't have a complete draft yet (the other two did), but that was okay. We were all focused on our writing and helping each other get it done, so even with differences in our approach, it felt like we had more than enough to work together on. It helped us stay motivated too. If you're thinking about planning your own retreat - whether for business or personal goals, I highly recommend it.

In the past year, I've been on a few writing retreats. They all have one thing in common: they're a great way to change up your scenery and get work done without distractions - especially if that scenery is somewhere you can walk around outside of your home. It's why we chose Philly for this trip. The other two were within driving distance, so it made it easy. We rented an Airbnb with enough bedrooms and bathrooms for each of us, plus a great writing table where we could spread out.

We also had some restaurants within walking distance, which was a big perk - no cooking or cleanup required while we focused on our writing projects. And exploring the city together added to our inspiration.

It's funny how getting away from home can make it feel easier to get work done - especially when that work is creative or requires focus. I'm going to keep doing retreats like this, because they help me stay accountable and motivated - which are two things a lot of us need (especially in business).

baseline.response:
The bliss of a solo road trip - just me, my trusty Honda CR-V, and the open road. We headed out to this secluded coastal town I've been wanting to explore for ages, with no particular agenda other than soaking up some sun, sea air, and maybe, just maybe, some writing magic.

As we pulled into our little beachside bungalow, I felt a familiar sense of excitement - like I was embarking on an adventure, not just unwinding. The first thing I did? Unplug from the world (no phone, no laptop) and let the sound of waves wash over me. It's crazy how quickly we get accustomed to the constant ping-ping-ping of notifications, isn't it?

I spent my days lounging on the beach, scribbling in my journal, and devouring books that sparked creativity (Big Magic by Elizabeth Gilbert was my trusty sidekick). I wrote some terrible first drafts - but what did I expect? This wasn't about producing perfection; it was about showing up to my own creative process with kindness.

One afternoon, as the sun began its lazy descent into the ocean, I decided to take a solo hike along the coast. The path was rugged, wild, and utterly unpretentious - exactly how I like it when I'm writing. I felt no need to tackle some grand, ambitious project; just me, my pen, and the wind in my hair.

And then, something magical happened: I started writing without thinking about it. No pressure, no deadlines, no expectations. Just words pouring out of me like sand between my toes. It was messy, imperfect - but also strangely free.

As I sat on that beach, watching the stars twinkle to life above, I felt a deep sense of trust in the process. Trust that the stories would come when they were ready; trust that I didn't need to force it; and trust that, no matter how rough the drafts might be, the magic was already within me.

So here's my question for you: What if you just... let go of all those expectations around your creative pursuits? Not even close to perfect, but present.

examples.prompt: Write a blog post titled "Jacqueline Fisch - Wishbeads Guest Blog post"
examples.reference:
I've been a long-time fan of Wishbeads bracelets. First, and selfishly, for myself - I mean, who wouldn't want another tool to help make their wishes come true?

And then, as a writer and writing coach, I wanted to keep finding ways to surprise and delight my clients. I mean, I can only send people so many pens and notebooks and what better way to celebrate the work my clients accomplish than to support them in making their wishes come true.

What's even more amazing is hearing the stories and seeing the pictures of the beads on people's social media feed. My clients often become my friends, and it's incredibly satisfying to deepen a friendship by honoring each other's wishes. I keep a few dozen Wishbeads in a variety of colors stashed under my desk to surprise and delight my clients.

As I package them up, gratitude and a knowing smile always wash over me as I imagine the look on their face, and then them making a wish, rolling it up, and slipping it inside the secret compartment. And of course, when their dreams become a reality.

Swapping out my wishpaper when my wish has come true brings me tremendous joy.

I have a few Wishbeads bracelets, and they all contain a different wish. There's something that happens when you intentionally focus on a specific outcome - you notice all the ways your wish is already on its way to you.

What's also beautiful is how your wish might show up in a slightly different form. It's an excellent reminder to let go of the how. I realized along this wishing journey, that the more I pay attention and look for signs of my wish out there, the faster wishes come true. There's actually some brain science to this too - it's called the reticular activating system (or the RAS) and our brain shows us what we're focusing on. Same reason that once you decide to go Jeep shopping you can't help but notice all the Jeeps on the road.

This past summer, I wanted to give the ladies in my mastermind group a special gift - something we could all connect with, and deepen our relationship. Having shared many hours of conversations, connections, tears, and advice with them over the past year, I wanted to gift them something meaningful.

I'm also unreasonably practical, so the idea of giving a gift that sits on a shelf or doesn't get used hurts my heart a little.

Inside our mastermind group, we all have big goals and important dreams.

What if we all had Wishbeads to cement our highest hopes for ourselves and our businesses?

Wanting to give each of these important women a color they'd love, I sent everyone a cryptic message the evening before my flight to meet them at our annual retreat, "Black, blue, or green?" The three colors of bracelets I had beneath my desk. I collected their responses, tucked their Wishbeads into my carry-on bag and boarded the plane.

Our theme for our most recent group mastermind retreat was rejuvenation, and we had just spent a delicious hour vibrating along with a sound bath experience. It was my first sound bath experience - you lay down on yoga mats while someone creates sounds using a variety of instruments, including tuning forks, gongs, crystal singing bowls, chimes, and voice.

The sound bath left us deeply relaxed, so now was the perfect time to present the gifts.

I called each member of our group over and presented a Wishbeads bracelet in their chosen color, which was followed by gasps of delight, gratitude, and hugs. Having been an experienced "wisher," I offered up some suggestions on the specific language to use when writing their wishes.

The results of our group all leaving that evening, making wishes, and seeing them wearing their intentions was potent.

Some ladies shared their wishes later with the group, and some kept them private.

Within a few weeks the updates started rolling in:

My wish came true already!

I crushed it! Time for a new wish!

My wish is coming true!

It ended up looking slightly different, but still amazing!

Wishing as a group was a powerful and moving experience. In our inner circle, we held each other accountable and were able to hold the space, and in a way, amplify one another's wishes. Having insight into each other's big intentions also helped us to serve as reminders for one another - for those of us who shared our wish, we now had five other women keeping their eyes and ears open for clues.

Wishing alone can take you far, wishing together can take you farther!

She's the creator of Write Like a MOFO, an online writing community for writers, coaches, and business owners. In this podcast, we chat about the power of setting intentions and goals and then doing some dang work to get it done! Learn more about Jacq here.

fine_tuned.response:
I am so excited to share this special guest blog post with you today! I was honored when Jacqueline Fisch asked me if I'd like to write for her amazing blog, and I'm thrilled that it's finally live.

As an intuitive writer coach, author, and podcast host (she has a fantastic show called "How Women Write"), Jacq is all about helping women build confidence in their writing. She shares practical tips and strategies to support you in getting your words out into the world - whether that means writing a book, launching a blog, or simply being more mindful of how you write.

This post is special because it's not just any old guest blog post. I get to share my own story with you, which includes how I went from having zero experience as a writer (let alone an intuitive one) to becoming the editor-in-chief for a national magazine and starting my own copywriting business. And along the way, I learned some valuable lessons about writing that I'm excited to pass on to you!

So without further ado, here's my guest blog post:

Wishbeads Guest Blog Post

I remember when I first started playing with wishbeads as a kid. It was all so magical - you'd make a wish and then put the bead in your pocket or somewhere safe, and then believe that it would come true!

Now, as an adult who's spent many years practicing intuitive writing (more on that later), I've realized that wishbeads aren't just for kids. The magic of making a wish is something we can all tap into - especially when it comes to our creative goals.

As someone who writes for a living and runs her own business, Jacq is no stranger to the world of writing. In fact, she's one of the most intuitive writers I know! She has a way of getting right to the point in every sentence, with no fluff or filler.

Intuitive writing - which means trusting your instincts and letting them guide you as you create - isn't something I was naturally good at when I started. I had to work hard at it, but it's been worth it many times over. When you write from a place of intuition, your words come alive on the page in a way that feels authentic and true to who you are.

I know there have been times when writing felt like the last thing you wanted to do. Maybe you're just getting started or maybe you've been at it for years - either way, I'm sure you can relate. It's easy to get caught up in trying to fix what we don't like about our writing (and who doesn't love a good edit?) but sometimes that means we lose sight of why we wanted to write in the first place.

So how do we find our flow again? For me, it comes back to intuition. When I'm stuck or frustrated with my writing, I take a step back and ask myself - what's really going on here?

Most often, I realize that I've been overthinking something or trying too hard instead of just letting the words fall out.

Intuitive Writing: A Practice for Your Creative Soul

There are many ways to practice intuitive writing. Here are some ideas:

Write morning pages (Julia Cameron style) and let your stream-of-consciousness flow onto the page. You might be surprised at what comes up!

Get quiet, calm, and centered - then write whatever is on your mind.

Use free-writing prompts that spark your creativity

Let go of any need for perfection and just start writing

Start with a small goal, like freewriting for 10 minutes or writing one page (or even a single sentence!)

I'm not going to tell you what to do - but I will suggest trying some of these ideas. It might feel uncomfortable at first - especially if you're used to pushing yourself in your writing. But trust me when I say that once you start letting go, things can shift quickly!

Intuitive Writing Tip #1: Let Go Of The Need To Fix Your Mistakes

I learned this one the hard way. When I was editing for a magazine, I would spend hours poring over articles and perfecting every word - until sometimes it just didn't feel fun anymore.

The truth is that no one wants to read perfect prose - we're all human! We make mistakes. And when you can let go of the need to fix those mistakes and instead focus on getting your words out, you'll be amazed at how quickly you can write.

Intuitive Writing Tip #2: Start Small

If you feel overwhelmed by a big project or want to get started with writing but don't know where to begin - start small. For me, that means freewriting for 10 minutes each morning (the more I do it the easier it gets!) and then letting whatever comes up guide my next steps.

Intuitive Writing Tip #3: Trust Your Instincts

This one goes hand-in-hand with starting small - but really is all about trusting your intuition. When you're writing, if a sentence or paragraph feels off to you, trust your gut and rewrite it! I know this can be scary for some of us (I certainly struggled at first), but when you keep going back to the page and asking yourself what's really going on - you'll find that you need less time and effort as you go along.

Intuitive Writing Tip #4: Stop Overthinking

If something isn't working, stop overthinking it. Sometimes we get stuck because we're trying too hard or second-guessing ourselves (or both!). When this happens to me, I take a step back, breathe, and start fresh - often with a new perspective.

Intuitive Writing Tip #5: Keep Going

This one's easy! Just keep going! Even if you don't feel like it in the moment - or even if you're not sure what you're writing about. Sometimes all we need is to get out of our own heads and onto the page (or screen!) so that we can see more clearly.

Intuitive Writing Tip #6: Read Aloud

I know this might sound silly, but when I'm editing my work or even just reading back over what I wrote - sometimes a fresh set of ears is all I need to get unstuck. I find it helpful to read my work aloud (this is especially true for the morning pages and freewriting) so that I can hear where I might be struggling.

Intuitive Writing Tip #7: Let Go Of Perfectionism

This one is easy too! Just let go of perfectionism - whether you're working on a big project or just writing something simple. When we focus on being perfect, it creates unnecessary pressure and stress around our creative process (which can lead to burnout!). When I was in the corporate world, I had a hard time letting go of this because there were so many stakeholders involved with every piece I wrote - but once I stepped out of that environment and into my own business, it got easier.

Intuitive Writing Tip #8: Keep Your Journal Close

This one is an old favorite of mine. When you're writing from the heart (which can sometimes feel like a vulnerable or scary place), it's nice to have something tangible to hold onto - especially if you're feeling uncertain about what you want to write about. I recommend keeping your journal close so that when those feelings come up, you can go back and explore them in a safe space.

Intuitive Writing Tip #9: Practice Mindfulness

This one is important for me because it keeps my creative process grounded. It's easy to get swept away by ideas and inspiration - but if I'm not mindful of where I am or what I need in that moment, I can burn out quickly! When you're writing from the heart (or head), it helps a lot to be present and fully engaged with your creative project.

Intuitive Writing Tip #10: Give Yourself Permission To Write Slowly

This one is hard for me because I know how much people love quick and easy solutions. But if there's one thing that I've learned over the years, it's this - writing doesn't need to be fast or slow; just whatever feels right in the moment.

Intuitive Writing Tip #11: Create Space For Your Creativity

This one is important because when you're creating something from scratch (which we all do whether we realize it or not), you need space for that creativity. This can mean taking a walk, meditating, or simply stepping away to clear your head before coming back to the task at hand.

Intuitive Writing Tip #12: Write Daily

I know this might be hard for some of us (because let's face it - who has time?) but if you want to stay in flow and keep creating from a place that feels authentic, write daily. If you can't make writing fit into your schedule, try freewriting for 10 minutes each morning or doing whatever works best for you.

Intuitive Writing Tip #13: Trust That Your Words Will Be Heard

This one is about trusting that when you put your words out there - they will be heard and have an impact. It's easy to get caught up in worrying if anyone will listen, but it doesn't matter! Because the truth is, our words are powerful, no matter what we choose to do with them.

Intuitive Writing Tip #14: Let Go Of Fear And Perfectionism

This one goes hand-in-hand with trusting your instincts - but really is about letting go of fear and perfectionism. When you're writing from a place of intuition (and not trying too hard or overthinking), it's so much easier to write without fear.

Intuitive Writing Tip #15: Embrace The Process

I know this one might be difficult for some because we often get caught up in wanting the end result instead of enjoying every step along the way. But when you can embrace the process - especially if that means being patient and allowing yourself time to figure things out, it makes writing so much easier!

**Wishbeads: A Love Letter to Your Inner Creative**

I'm sitting here with my morning coffee, surrounded by the quiet of my home office, and I'm thinking about the things we tell ourselves. You know, those pesky inner dialogues that whisper "you're not good enough" or "this will never work out." The ones that make us doubt our worth, our abilities, and our dreams.

And then I think about Wishbeads.

If you've been following along with me on this writing journey, you might know that I'm a sucker for metaphors. And what's more metaphorical than these tiny, stringed orbs of possibility? Each one representing a hope, a desire, or a prayer – tied to a thread of intention and sent out into the universe.

I've been collecting Wishbeads for years now, each one a tangible reminder that my creative dreams are worth holding onto. I have them strung around my writing desk like a garland of good vibes. They're a visual manifestation of the things I'm working towards – a published book, a successful podcast, a life filled with words and wonder.

But here's the thing: Wishbeads aren't just for big dreams. They're for small ones too – the desire to write 500 words without interruption, or to finish that pesky chapter you've been putting off for months. They're for the everyday moments of creative resistance we all face.

And I think this is where the magic happens. When we tie a Wishbead around our wrist (or in my case, around my writing desk), we're making a promise to ourselves – a promise to show up, to trust the process, and to believe in our own inner creative genius.

So what if you just... started collecting your own Wishbeads? What if you took the time to string together a few beads that represent your deepest desires, and tied them around your neck like a badge of honor? What would happen then?

For me, it's been a game-changer. A reminder that my creative dreams are worth fighting for – even when the inner critic starts to whisper "you're not good enough." Even when the doubts creep in and the resistance builds.

So go ahead, take out your Wishbeads (or grab some string and beads from the craft store). Let's make a pact to support each other in our creative endeavors. To show up, to trust ourselves, and to believe that we're capable of producing something truly amazing.

And as you tie on those first few beads – remember: it's not about the destination; it's about the journey. It's about the promise you make to yourself every time you pick up your pen or sit down at your desk.

**Your turn:** What are some Wishbeads you'd like to add to your string? What creative dreams do you need a reminder to chase after?

examples.prompt: Describe the writing process as a journey of intentional motion and growth, where one's body of work is carefully curated to nourish and delight their audience.
examples.reference:
An object in motion tends to stay in motion. That's our goal here - motion. And not just any kind of motion: Intentional, grounded, and aligned motion to support your highest path. There will be bumps and it will be uncomfortable at times. The more you embrace discomfort and feel okay in it, the more you'll grow as a writer and as a person. Buckle up and enjoy the ride.

Chapter 20: Your body of work

In Chapter 13 we talked about your charcuterie board of content. If you're worried your content, books, and blog posts all read like a mishmash of topics, I invite you to think about everything you write about as a component that goes on one of these tastebud adventures on a wooden plank.

The tastes of the charcuterie board are delicious when enjoyed alone and even better when paired together. And with a delightfully curated presentation, it's also a feast for your eyes.

Applying the charcuterie concept to your writing, each of your blog posts, courses, and books are nourishing and helpful on its own. When enjoyed one after another with a small taste from all the other accompanying content - the experience is even better.

Your group programs paired with your weekly emails.

Your books paired with your newsletter.

Your social media captions paired with your poetry.

Your podcast paired with your workshops.

This is why organizing your content into buckets, or a handful of key themes, will help your work appear as a charcuterie board. You wouldn't drop mashed potatoes, Brussels sprouts, or spaghetti on your board. It wouldn't fit.

And now, if you're wondering how many themes you have or how even to begin organizing your body of work, here are some ways to figure them out.

Remember that each piece of writing you create is not for everyone. Think about the writers you enjoy - do you absolutely love every bit of their work? Maybe. But chances are that you enjoy some pieces more than others.

Just like the plant-based eater at your party, they're not going to be mad if there's prosciutto on your plate - they'll just eat the things they prefer.

1. Scan your existing writing.

Look across everything you've ever written. If you had to put all the topics into categories, where would they naturally fit? Then, consider your list of groups and think about combining or removing some if there are too many. Are there some topics you no longer write about? You can let those live on the internet and write about things you're most interested in now. If you notice you have a single theme, that's great too. You can absolutely stick to one subject or add more if you feel inspired in the months and years ahead.

2. Consider what you want to be known for.

How do you want people to remember you? Do you want them to remember you for your ability to tell a story and apply a life lesson? Do you want to be known for your no-BS talk? What subject matter do you want readers to associate with your name? Do you want to be a poet who cooks? A romance writer who's also a kickass marketer?

3. Consider what you could talk about all day long.

When you talk about something, and it brings you an inner feeling of excitement and enthusiasm, that right there is magic - follow it. Remember, what feels good to you to create feels good to your readers. List out all the topics that make you feel antsy with anticipation. Subjects so good that you can't wait to share what you know.

4. What do people always ask for your advice on?

If people constantly ask you for cooking advice and you're a life coach, you may want to keep those topics to a minimum. When you notice you're giving the same advice repeatedly, you've got a gift right there. That's the magic sauce you can serve to your audience.

Intuitive Writing Tip

Uncouple the idea that everything you write needs to make money. Some writing can (and should) be for fun or for helping others. Let your art be art without putting pressure on it to do something for you - like make you famous or rake in a million bucks.

5. Write out your list of themes - maybe you have one, three, or even six.

Review each topic on the list. Do you enjoy and want to continue talking about these topics? If not, go ahead and strike some. Maybe what you remove from your blog content is what you'll share in your books. And if you do love these topics, now you have some boundaries to help you brainstorm.

fine_tuned.response:
You want me to describe the writing process like this?

The writing process is not a linear destination - but rather an ever-unfolding journey.

It's about showing up on the page with open hands, allowing words to flow freely without resistance or ego-driven control. Like creating a charcuterie board of content, each piece you write is a delicious morsel that adds depth and richness to your body of work when paired together.

As you create intentionally, each writing experience becomes an opportunity for growth and transformation - just like how athletes move into flow state when fully immersed in their sport. You're not writing for others - but instead, you're serving the words that want to come through you. And as you surrender to this process, you'll get the guidance you need.

The result? Your body of work is a nourishing and delightful collection of writings, courses, books, and more - each one serving a specific purpose and connecting with your audience in its own unique way.

It's about embracing discomfort and staying aligned with your highest path. When you're fully present in the flow state, ego can't run the show - allowing for true freedom to write what wants to come through you. And when you publish, you're not just sharing your words - but serving up a tastebud adventure that delights and nourishes.

You'll be surprised at how quickly you grow as a writer and as a person when you move into intentional motion with an open mind and heart. It's really about trusting the process, having faith in yourself, and surrendering to what wants to come through - and that's where the magic happens.

It might feel like getting out of your comfort zone is uncomfortable at times (and it can be) but remember that you're doing this for a reason: growth. Growth as a person, growth as a writer. When we're in flow state, we're fully immersed, and our creativity and ideas can shine through without any blocks or resistance.

And the end result? You'll have written something that truly feels like your own - something you can stand behind with pride.

The most important thing to keep in mind is that writing doesn't have to be a struggle. And when it is - that's usually because we're trying too hard, overthinking, or letting our ego get in the way.

It's about being open-minded and available for whatever wants to come through you. Trusting your intuition will serve as your guide, and staying aligned with what feels true to you can keep you grounded.

You'll know it when you're in flow - you might even wonder where time went because you were so fully immersed. And that's the point: getting lost in the writing process is not a bad thing. It's good! It means you're fully engaged, and your words are flowing from a place of truth and honesty.

When I think about how people approach writing (and their businesses) - it's often with resistance, stress, pressure, or force. Not fun! And when we try to push through something that feels uncomfortable, we may create more resistance than we started out with.

The key is to surrender to what wants to come through you and trust the process. It might feel like a slow unfolding at first - but that's okay! You're building strength, not speed. Your words are going to flow in perfect timing when you show up on the page with an open heart and mind.

And if you think you have a book or blog post "someday" - it could come sooner than you imagine. When you surrender and trust that process (and yourself), magic can happen, and your writing will transform in ways you never thought possible.

examples.prompt: Describe the strategies you use to manage your thoughts and emotions when faced with negative self-talk or criticism from others.
examples.reference:
One of my favorites is the cancel process. While I'm usually thoughtful with my words, sometimes my mouth moves faster than my brain, and I say something stupid or out of line. Wait! I take that back! Well, I do the same with my thinking. When a thought creeps up that dulls my shine, I say something in my head like cancel, cancel, cancel, or delete, delete, delete, or reject and delete.

Then, I get to tell a better story.

You are in charge of your thoughts. You can change them whenever you like. Hear a load of crap in your ear? Stop that thought and tell yourself another one. You don't have to be all fairy dust about it and tell yourself that you're a sparkly rainbow of luminous delight. Use words you'd use with close friends, like "Hey, this is completely normal, do better next time."

I also have a thank bank that I deposit praise, kind emails, and kudos into regularly. I keep mine in a Google Doc and in a special email folder that I literally named "Thank Bank." Every time I get a gushing email, or a small note of thanks or praise, I take a copy and store it in my thank bank. On days when my thoughts are running wild, I make a mistake, or actually do suck at something, I read through my thank bank and feel so much better.

Beyond showering myself in gratitude, sharing gratitude outward gives me an instant boost. Sometimes I write a thank-you note on a card and drop it in the mail, send a nice email, or write a review of a favorite restaurant.

In addition, consider getting a coach, a therapist, and some friends who will check your limiting thoughts at the door. Even better, get all three - remember, you become the essence of the five people you spend the most time with.

For most of my life I tried to go it alone. The lone warrior, I always had an idea that asking for help made me weak. I'm still unraveling those behaviors. The more you ask for help, the more you realize that people want to help. Especially when those people are good at it.

Here's what my support network looks like these days - Ryan holding up the other end of our household in his career, striving for more and better, and pushing me to be better. My kids to show me where I need to work on myself, and for them to do chores around the house (for this reason, a housekeeper is not yet part of my support system). A paid mastermind because I don't want to be the smartest person in a room. Business besties - friends I can text in the middle of the day when I'm stuck or need to bounce my latest idea off someone. A coach - who's supported me in wiping out negative emotions (yeah, I didn't think it was possible either) and showing me how to train my brain to expect good things. My work with my coach has been a game changer. My family, who I can always count on. Even though we're five hundred miles away, they're my number one fans.

An objective opinion will be able to spot a negative thought spiral a mile away and can help you reframe it. And it takes practice. I've spent more than a decade consciously looking at my thoughts, and there's still some gunk that comes up in there from time to time. It's going to happen - I can use the opportunity to strengthen my mind, and over time, it becomes a habit.

You've got a masterpiece to create

When I was first thinking about starting a business - remember, eight whole years before I had enough momentum to take it full time, I spent months trying to figure it out by staring at my computer, filling out worksheets, and downloading every free PDF on the internet - I was doing a lot of things that made me feel like I was building something but didn't actually take me any further down the road. I was desperate to finally figure out what it was that I wanted to do with my life. It was as if someone else or something outside of me had all the answers.

Night after night, I'd eat an inappropriate amount of chocolate and drink just a little more wine than I'd planned. I'd have an idea - I'm going to only write about hummus! I wish I was joking. I thought I was going to build a business around dip. Now, I believe you can create a business around just about anything. Building a thriving income around dip might have been a stretch, though.

fine_tuned.response:
Managing my thoughts and emotions - especially when faced with negative self-talk or criticism from others. It's a topic I'm super passionate about because I've been there, done that, and have some favorite tools for navigating the noise.

First off, let's talk about how to stop that chatter in your head. When you hear yourself thinking something crappy, it's time to pause - like literally hit pause on the thought. You can say out loud or to yourself, "Cancel," "delete," or "reject and delete." Yes, these are actual words I use! If a negative thought pops up, stop and tell yourself a better story. You get to choose what you think.

Now, when someone else is critical of me - whether it's a family member, friend, or client who says something hurtful - it can be challenging not to internalize those comments. But here's the thing: if I'm listening too closely, and taking it personally, that critic is winning. When I hear criticism, I immediately go into my "thank bank" (more on this in a minute). This is when I remind myself of all the people who believe in me and appreciate what I do.

The thank bank is where I store notes of praise from readers, emails, or texts that make me feel good. It's like having an instant confidence boost when you need it most. When someone else criticizes me, I go to my thank bank and read through all the amazing things people have said about me - and that usually shuts down the negative chatter.

And if criticism is coming from within, such as in the form of self-doubt or fear? Well, first, I want you to know that you're not alone. Every single person experiences this. And here's what works for me: when I feel anxious, scared, or uncertain, I get quiet and listen. Before my brain starts telling me all the things I can't do or why I'll never make it happen, I stop and ask myself questions.

If you're feeling crappy from a rejection letter or because someone said no to working with you, try not to leap directly into joy (which is often too big of a jump anyway). Instead, see if you can get comfortable - like literally feel your body relax. Then, go from there. When I'm in that state and feeling crappier than a dumpster fire, I look to see what's really going on. What am I afraid of? Is it something specific I can change? Am I putting too much pressure on myself?

And if you're feeling stuck or uncertain about your next steps - whether it's writing an article, making a phone call, or sending that email - remember that getting quiet and listening is always the answer.

When you get quiet and tune into what your body wants to tell you, you can make tiny tweaks to feel better. Sometimes this means pausing on social media for a few hours, taking a walk outside, writing some morning pages (more on this below), or even just putting down your phone.

In my own life, I've learned that it's only when I let go of the need to control everything and give myself permission to make mistakes that I truly feel free. And that freedom feels amazing! When you're living in a state of constant stress - whether from criticism, fear, or anxiety - you can't create anything good for yourself.

So, what does it all come down to? It's not about changing your external circumstances but transforming your inner state. That takes some work up front, but it sets you up for life-changing results later on.

baseline.response:
You know how it goes - that voice in our heads, always ready to rip us apart. I'm talking about the one that says we're not good enough, that our writing is crap, that we're just pretending to be authors. It's like having a constant critic on speed dial.

First off, let me say this: most of what we tell ourselves is total nonsense. We'd never speak to our friends or family members with the same level of cruelty as we do to ourselves. So, why are we so gentle with others and not with ourselves?

Okay, here's my go-to move when I'm caught in the vortex of self-doubt: I take a step back - literally and figuratively. I stop whatever I'm doing and breathe. Like, really breathe. Not just that shallow stuff either; I'm talking deep, belly-expanding breaths.

And then, I remind myself of what Elizabeth Gilbert said: "You're not a writer because you write; you write because you're a writer." Repeat it like a mantra if you need to - it's about identity, not some external validation from others. We are writers because we have something to say, and that's worth trusting.

When criticism comes from outside, I try to separate the wheat from the chaff. What can I learn from this feedback? Is there any truth in what they're saying? Can I use it as fuel for growth or is it just a bunch of toxic nonsense?

Not even close. I mean, have you ever tried to "improve" your writing based on someone else's opinion? It's like trying to follow a recipe written by someone who hates baking.

So, here's what I do instead: I take the criticism and use it as a springboard for self-inquiry. What am I afraid of? What part of me is feeling vulnerable right now? Because usually, that's where the real growth happens - not in trying to fix our writing based on someone else's feedback, but in facing our own fears and doubts head-on.

Just that. Sometimes, it takes just acknowledging what's going on inside us for things to shift. And when we do finally start to trust ourselves and our writing, watch out world. That's when the real magic happens.

Sound familiar? We've all been there - lost in a sea of self-doubt and criticism. But here's the thing: it's not about being "good enough" or meeting someone else's standards. It's about showing up authentically, with intention and heart. That's what writing is all about.

examples.reference:
I love a good writing prompt. Sometimes I look at writing prompts, especially when they're in a prescribed format when I have to follow them in order, and they feel stifling.

Or, I read a prompt, ready to dive into my unconscious mind to see what wants to come out, and it just feels off. I can't explain the feeling, but it doesn't feel exciting to write about.

A few years ago, I started aligning my content writing calendar with the astrological calendar.

So why not have a set of writing prompts to explore during every astrological season?

How to use these prompts:

Save this article to come back to throughout the year and visit each sun sign's writing prompts as we move from season to season.

Choose to ponder, write about, or explore one prompt a week for the season.

Have fun with them and come back to them.

You can use these to drive content inspiration for your business, or writing ideas for your blog and emails - just use them.

Play with the writing prompts for your sun, rising, and moon signs. Don't know what those are? Find out for free on astro.com with your date, time, and place of birth, or join my favorite astrologer's community - Luminary Parenting.

Aries: The Ram

Dates: Mar 21 - Apr 19

Mode + Element: Cardinal Fire

What lights your fire?

Write 5-10 "I am..." statements in positive toward language.

Hold a ceremony to initiate yourself as a writer.

Write out the details of your most abundant launch. How do people feel signing up? Where are you? How do you celebrate?

During what event did you feel truly fearless? Where is that energy needed today?

Taurus: The Bull

Dates: Apr 20 - May 20

Mode + Element: Fixed Earth

List 10 things you have - Can be material goods, strengths, or qualities that help you feel safe, secure, and at peace.

How can you serve your current customers ever more powerfully?

Review your body of work. Write gratitude for your favorite creations.

Put your bare feet on the ground and ask, who am I?

Review your list of unfinished projects. Choose one project and a target completion date. What resources do you need to finish it? Who can help? Write it on your calendar and plan your finishing strategy.

Gemini: The Twins

Dates: May 21 - Jun 20

Mode + Element: Mutable Air

What do you want to know from your audience? Send an email and ask them.

Write your argument for what you believe. Now get curious, and write the opposite.

What difficult conversation do you need to have? Write what you'll say in an email or letter. Only send it if you want to.

Describe the way you'd like to improve your communication.

Describe your favorite co-creation with the Divine.

Cancer: The Crab

Dates: June 21 - July 22

Mode + Element: Cardinal Water

Write about how you feel around bodies of water.

Where can you nurture your inner writer more deeply?

How do your clients like to be nurtured?

Go above and beyond to nurture your next prospective client. What do you do?

Create a survey for your customers with all the things you'd love to know but were afraid to ask.

Reconnect with a past customer or your email list.

Leo: The Lion

Dates: July 23 - Aug 22

Declare what you'll do this month in a series of "I will" statements.

Where do you crave more visibility? Where can you promote yourself?

Plan your next launch with all the bold details, including how you'll celebrate.

Commit a day this week to looking for sparks.

Plan the rest of your year.

Is your authentic self showing up in your writing?

Where are you called to go BIG?

Virgo: The Virgin

Dates: Aug 23 - Sep 22

Mode + Element: Mutable Earth

What's your favorite way to serve?

Ask your readers, "How can I best serve you today?"

Go through all your old notes and files. What feels valuable to pay more attention to?

How do you feel about your current content format (blogging, podcasting, social media posting)? What else could you explore?

List out your strengths in your teaching style.

Look at your well-being routine. Think about where it needs an upgrade.

Libra: The Scales

Dates: Sep 23 - Oct 22

Mode + Element: Cardinal Air

If you could collaborate with your favorite creative or business person, what would you make together?

How can you improve your relationship with your reader?

Where are you craving more balance?

Revisit your writing space and make some small upgrades to make it more inviting.

Craft a vision board of your dreamiest writing retreat.

List your most trusted business friends. Do they fit where you're going? Is there anyone missing?

Scorpio: The Scorpion

Dates: Oct 23 - Nov 21

Mode + Element: Fixed Water

Review the parts of your client's lives that you change and write 10 declarations starting with, "I transform..."

Share a case study of a client transformation.

Write about a transformation you've been through how you achieved it.

What work is calling for you to put your head down and go deep?

What will you commit to?

How could you put yourself out there (even if it feels scary) just to see what happens?

Sagittarius: The Archer

Dates: Nov 22 - Dec 21

Mode + Element: Mutable Fire

Think about your next writing project and what you see it doing for your reader. Commit these to a series of "I see" statements.

Allow yourself to dream big - what does it look like?

What wisdom do you love to share? How can you share more?

Where can you take three new risks in your business or writing?

Create your vision for the coming year. Then 5 years from now. Pretend it's here today.

Capricorn: The Goat

Dates: Dec 22 - Jan 19

Mode + Element: Cardinal Earth

Plan the next three months of your writing and map out a plan to get it done.

What are people always asking for your advice on? Write a how-to blog or email.

Create your (unfussy) content plan for the coming year.

Take your big dreams and create a to-do list. Then plot out when you'll take action on these items.

Aquarius: The Water Bearer

Dates: Jan 20 - Feb 18

Mode + Element: Fixed Air

What's my favorite invention?

Where would it be fun to push boundaries?

If I could write anything at all, what would that be?

Choose a completely unrelated industry and go exploring. You never knew what you might find that would relate to your business or writing project.

Look at your recent content. Did you ask readers to share? Add a CTA to your most recent articles and your next newsletter.

I know this to be true...

Pisces: The Fish

Dates: Feb 19 - Mar 20

Mode + Element: Mutable Water

I used to believe__________. And today I believe___________.

Search images of your favorite artist, make one piece your computer or phone's background.

Bring to mind the last time you were in the flow of writing. How did it feel? Where were you? What did you create?

Where does your inner writer need healing?

Plan your dream writing retreat.

examples.prompt: Write a blog post titled "What if it was easy"
examples.reference:
I've been thinking a lot lately about the create-on-demand process that's a huge part of my work.

My clients rely on me to pull some clear and crisp writing out of my head and get it onto the page. And there's almost always a deadline.

Even this blog for instance. I've been blogging consistently since I don't remember when. Usually, subscribers get a weekly email from me on Tuesday, occasionally Wednesday.

And this week, the topic is also what I most needed to hear.

What if it was easy?

Have you ever been in the flow of writing and it just felt easy, natural, effortless. Dare I say, you were even having fun?

Maybe you weren't having any kind of feelings at all. You just sat down and started writing, and it came out of you.

Your energy was in the right place and it just fell out. Like it was waiting right there on your fingertips for the right opportunity for you to get out of your head for five minutes and just write.

What if writing was easy?

What would your blog look like if blogging was easy?

Would you sit down to write each week and just let the words flow out of you? Would you go back and forth 90 billion times about what topic to write about?

Or, could it just be easy?

What would your newsletter look like if it was easy?

What would a batch writing session look like if it was easy?

What would you be drinking?

What will you wear?

Where will you sit?

What's your posture like?

What's playing through your speakers?

What essential oil is being infused into the air?

What does your face look like? Crunched up or relaxed?

What's all around you?

You see, when it's easy, it's faster. When it's easy, it feels good. When it's easy, it just works.

Easy get's noticed. Not because you took the direct path, but because your energy was there. The energy you brought to the page was clean and open and ready to spill over.

Your readers notice.

They notice the way you make them feel.

So why the hell shouldn't it be easy?

The next time you're stuck in a shit sandwich or staring down a task you just don't want to start before you begin, ask this question....

What would it look like if it was easy?

Begin there.

I love my clients' faces when they look at their finished piece of work - whether that's an email newsletter, a blog post, or even just some copy for a social media ad. They're often so proud and relieved.

And sometimes, they ask me, "How do you make it seem so effortless?"

The answer? It's not effortless. I'm not writing this for fun (though I love what I do). And I'm definitely not getting paid to write about how easy it is (that's just not the case).

What I am doing though - and what we're going to talk about in this article - is making writing feel easier.

And when I say "easier," I mean:

I don't have writer's block. Ever.

My blog posts flow out of me like water. They write themselves (well, almost).

The words just come out.

It feels good.

That's what I'm going to teach you - how to make writing feel easier for yourself, too.

And it starts with one simple question: what if it was easy?

What would your blog look like if blogging was easy? Would you sit down to write each week and just let the words flow out of you? Would you go back and forth 90 billion times about what topic to write about? Or could it just be easy?

Let me tell you a story. When I first started my business, I had two kids at home - ages 1 and 3. And yes, they were adorable, but they also required attention.

I was working on getting my website up and running (more on that another time) and I would write in the morning while my youngest slept next to me in her crib. She'd be sleeping, and I'd start writing - and just like magic, words would flow out of me.

It felt easy. It felt good. And it still does today.

Of course, not every day is perfect. Some days are harder than others (and some mornings are more challenging than others). But the point is that writing doesn't have to feel hard. And when you make it feel easier - whether you're a full-time writer or just blogging on the side - you'll be able to write with greater ease and flow.

And not only will your words get out there, but they'll also be more effective. When you're in the zone and writing feels easy, you can tap into that natural creative energy.

Your readers notice. They notice when a writer is being authentic and present - when their words feel like they're coming from the heart (and not just trying to say something they think someone else wants them to say).

So what if it was easy? What would your writing look like?

Would you post more often, or share more content on social media?

Could you sit down to write and let the words flow out of you?

What if blogging felt good? Would you do it more often?

The next time you're stuck in a shit sandwich (yes, I said that) or staring down a task you just don't want to start before you begin, ask yourself this question:

What would it look like if it was easy?

Begin there.

And remember: the first few words of your blog post are part of your first impression. It's what makes your reader want to keep reading.

So, I hope these suggestions help! If you're looking for even more ideas on how to start your next article with a killer opening line, check out this article here.

Happy writing!

And remember: the words just need to come out.

Finding Your Way: A Guide to Getting Back on Track

I'm not sure where you are right now. Maybe you're feeling stuck, like you've been walking in circles and haven't made any progress. Or maybe you're just feeling lost - without a clear direction or sense of purpose.

Either way, you're here because you need a nudge to get going again. And that's okay.

We all have those moments where we feel like we're wandering aimlessly through our lives. We may not know what we want or why we want it, and sometimes we just don't give ourselves permission to take the time to figure it out.

I've been there myself - many times. As a writer and business owner, I know how hard it can be to sit down with a blank page in front of you, feeling like you have nothing to say.

But here's what works for me: when I'm feeling stuck or lost, I go back to basics. I ask myself simple questions that cut through all the noise - and get straight to the point:

What do I love doing?

What makes me feel alive?

What lights me up? What gets me excited?

These are easy questions to answer. And sometimes, you may be surprised by how quickly you can come up with some answers.

I'm a big believer in starting small. When you're feeling stuck, it's often because you've been looking at the bigger picture for so long that you don't know where to start. Start with something simple - like making a plan for what you want to do tomorrow or next week. Having something concrete to work towards can help break up the overwhelm and make progress feel more manageable.

And when I say "start small," I mean really, really small. Maybe it's as simple as getting out of bed at 6:00 am instead of 7:00 am. Or maybe it means just spending five minutes a day working on your writing project.

That might not sound like much - but those little moments add up fast. And the more you do them, the easier they become to fit into your life and routine.

I know that I can sometimes overthink things too much. In my head, I'll go round and round with ideas, trying to figure out every single detail before taking action. But in reality, most of these details don't matter as much as you think. What matters is just showing up - and doing something.

One thing that works for me when I'm stuck or feeling lost is getting back into a routine. When you're not used to doing something regularly, it can feel like trying to do the impossible. But if you get yourself on a schedule, then you'll have something solid to work with - and it will become easier over time.

I've been blogging for years now. And I know that every week when I sit down to write a new post, I'm not always excited about what I'm going to write. Sometimes the inspiration just isn't there - but I still show up at my desk and get started anyway.

And you know what? Every single time I've done this, something amazing has come out of it. Not every time will be brilliant - but some of those posts have been life-changing for people who read them. And that's a reminder to me to just keep going - even when it feels like the work is hard or I'm struggling.

So if you're feeling stuck or lost, know that this too shall pass. You are not your circumstances right now - and they will change. You have the power to choose how you feel and what you do about those feelings. And you can start making changes today.

Here's a simple question for you: What one thing could you do today that would make your life easier tomorrow?

Do it. Just take the first step, and then see where it takes you.

## things I learned from working in crisis communications for three years

From 2009 until 2012 I worked for a major telecommunications company. BlackBerry, maybe you've heard of them? Back then, they were called RIM. Yes, I had RIM-job (had to).

Even though most of us are wielding iPhones now... you know from the other fruit company (not me though, Android all the way because Apple was the enemy back in those BlueBerry days.

Back in 2009 though, BlackBerry was in its heyday. My first job interviews them was the day after their private Van Halen concert. Well, shit, this company sounds pretty cool.

I landed my RIM-job shortly after getting laid off for the first time from a Chicago-based management consulting company. I am grateful for everything the company did... like relocated our family of three at the time from Chicago to the Greater Toronto Area and made sure everything was smooth.

This was my first formal communications job. Sure, I had written tons up until this point. Proposals worth millions of dollars, emails worth even more, but they had rules and process, and it was there that I really started paying attention to word choice.

The experiences I had at BlackBerry serve me today. When you mess up and send the wrong thing to the wrong client when poo flies sideways, and you need to smooth it over.

While I was working there, anytime the BlackBerry service would go down, for any wireless carrier in the world; it was my job to talk to the engineers, find out what happened, and turn around a perfectly polished statement. It had to happen usually within a day, and if it didn't, it could cost the company money.

Here are ## key lessons that could serve you in your business:

Use the language of possibility. For example, instead of saying, "Customers couldn't surf the internet." You'd say, "Customer might not have been able to use the internet." Do you see the difference?

Focus on the positive. What did you learn from screwing up? In systems speak, we'd say something like, "Now that we've identified this, we're working to add additional system components to make it stronger to avoid it happening in the future."

Don't use the F-word. That would be failure. Ack! How dirty is the word failure? Use this word enough, such as calling yourself the F-word, or saying you feel like one. Keep saying that out loud enough, and people will start to believe it.

Keep the blame. Sometimes BlackBerry service would go down, but it was a problem on a carrier's end. We wouldn't say, "It's your fault bucko - piss off." Incorporating language of possibility and keeping it positive, we'd say something like, "Users might have experienced problem using this feature for 10 minutes and we haven't determined the source of the problem."

When you don't know something, say so. Never make something up. It's okay just to say you don't know.

Be specific. Details matter. Don't say, "I'm a screw-up" say, "I screwed that response up." Put the focus on the thing, NOT on you.

Don't apologize. This is a tricky one, and if you've truly messed up, don't be an asshat - just say, "I'm sorry." Instead, turn it around to be positive. Like, if you missed a deadline or something is taking longer to deliver than you promised, or you've taken two weeks to answer an email, try, "Thank you for your patience." See the difference? Repeat after me, "Thank. You. For. Your. Patience." Instead of placing focus on the F-up that you made, focus on them while you're complimenting them for their patients.

Keep it short. Brevity is the key to my heart. When it comes to communicating a crisis, if you drone on and on, you're just going to make people angrier. Keep it simple, edit out extra words ruthlessly.

Proofread, proofread, proofread. When you're communicating a craptastic situation, it needs to be correct. I like to call this flawless execution. It's one of the handful of times in my life being a perfectionist really serves me well. Print it out, have a friend review it. At BlackBerry, at least five people touched each statement. An engineer to make sure we weren't talking out of our ass and to make sure we weren't sharing proprietary information, a peer, and two senior folks. It had to be on time, and it had to be right.

Words always matter. They matter even more when poo is on the ceiling. Take

Crisis Comms Like You Mean It: Why You Need To Get Your Message Out There And Not Just Let The Crisis Happen

You're in the middle of something. Something is happening, and you need to talk about it.

And I'm not talking about a crisis that's someone else's problem - or even one that you feel like you can sit back on the couch and watch unfold while eating potato chips.

I mean your crisis. The one that happened right now. Maybe you're in the middle of something - a personal health crisis, an unexpected business interruption, or even just a situation at work where everyone's freaked out and needs to know what's going on.

You need to tell people something. And not only do you need to get it said but you also want to do so in a way that doesn't make things worse. You probably don't have time for a lot of planning, thinking about all the possible ways your message could be misinterpreted, or even just coming up with the right words.

You're too busy getting through this thing and helping those who are affected to deal with whatever is going on. And that's exactly why you need to get your message out there - like now. You don't have time for anything else, so let's talk about how to communicate clearly during a crisis situation without making things worse.

First, tell people what's happening. If something bad has happened, then it needs to be said. Not in the details or even necessarily with all the facts but at least with the basics - "there is an issue" or "something happened." You can start there and go from there. It doesn't have to be a super long message either.

Next, make sure you're communicating clearly. This will help people feel more secure and confident in what's happening. When someone says that they don't know something, it might not always mean that they're trying to hide information - sometimes it means just that they haven't heard yet or even that there is no information available.

Tell your audience what you do know, when you'll have more updates (if appropriate), and how this situation will be addressed. It's also important to let people know who to contact if they need specific help or support. And remember - a message doesn't always have to be long. Sometimes it just needs to be said.

Here are some tips for writing your crisis comms:

1. Be clear about what happened. This can be as simple as saying, "there is an issue" and then going from there.

2. If you don't know something, say so. It's much better to tell people that you're not sure rather than pretend like you have the answer all figured out.

3. Let your audience know how this situation will be addressed - what steps you'll take or who they can contact for help.

4. Keep it concise. You don't need a long message. Sometimes, just saying something is enough.

5. Use plain language. Avoid using corporate jargon like "optimize" or "leverage." Just tell people what's happening in simple words that everyone can understand.

6. Be authentic and transparent - no one likes surprises when it comes to bad news. If you're unsure of something, say so.

7. Let your audience know who to contact if they need specific help or support.

8. Consider video as an alternative to written updates. People are visual (and we know that) so using a quick video update can be helpful too - especially when it comes to showing what's happening and making people feel like they're not alone in this thing.

9. Remember - crisis comms is about getting your message out there quickly, but also being empathetic and compassionate during the process. You might need to repeat yourself as many times as it takes for everyone to understand. It's a good idea to have someone help you craft a clear message that you can communicate consistently over time.

10. Once this situation has passed, you'll be back to your regular communication habits - but don't forget that crisis comms is an important part of being authentic and transparent in business and life. Remember what happened during the crisis (but only if it was relevant) and how you communicated through it so that when something else comes up, you can use those lessons as a guide to communicate effectively.

Crisis comms isn't something that happens by accident or that you should be worried about - although it's nice to have a plan for everything. Instead, think of crisis comms like a special project that requires you to act fast and get the message out there quickly while also being empathetic and compassionate during the process.

When you're in the middle of something, and you need to talk about what's happening - whether it's good or bad news, don't panic. Just tell people what's going on, when they can expect more updates (if appropriate), who to contact if they need help, and how this situation will be addressed.

And remember: in crisis comms, the goal is not just to get your message out there quickly but also to do so with empathy and compassion for those affected. You might need to repeat yourself as many times as it takes for everyone to understand - but that's okay. The more you communicate clearly during a crisis situation, the less scary or uncertain people will feel.

And don't forget: if you ever need help crafting a clear message in a crisis, please don't hesitate to reach out! I am here for you!

How to write your own crisis comms:

1. What happened? Tell people what's happening - but keep it concise.
2. Why is this important? Explain why it matters and who will be affected.
3. What are you doing about it? Let people know how this situation will be addressed.
4. Who can they contact for help? Make sure to include any relevant details, like a phone number or email address.
5. When can they expect updates? If there's more information coming later (like when the crisis is resolved), tell them.

Crisis comms isn't something that should make you feel anxious or overwhelmed. It's an opportunity for you to communicate clearly and show empathy during difficult times - whether it's with your team, customers, clients, friends, family, or even just yourself.

Being proactive (not reactive)

Transparency is key
